perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall use the same care and discretion to avoid disclosure, publication or dissemination of the Confidential Information as it uses with its own similar information that it does not wish to disclose, publish or disseminate ;
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall endeavor to use reasonable care and discretion to avoid disclosure, publication or dissemination of the Confidential Information, potentially similar to how it handles its own information it doesn't prefer to disclose, publish, or disseminate; however, the level of care is not guaranteed to be identical.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall use the same care and discretion" to "shall endeavor to use reasonable care and discretion, potentially similar to how it handles its own information" introduces ambiguity. "Reasonable care" is subjective, and the qualifier that the level is 'not guaranteed to be identical' weakens the obligation to protect the Confidential Information with the same rigor as the Recipient's own sensitive data.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification potentially contradicts the Trade Secrets Act, which protects trade secrets from misappropriation. By using ambiguous language such as 'endeavor' and explicitly stating that the level of care is 'not guaranteed to be identical,' the agreement might fail to provide sufficient protection to UNHCR's Confidential Information. The Trade Secret Act has no space for “endeavors”, the steps must be taken and followed strictly.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, which could contradict the spirit of laws protecting trade secrets. The original text mandates "the same care and discretion," while the changed text uses "endeavor to use reasonable care and discretion" and clarifies that the level of care is "not guaranteed to be identical." This introduces ambiguity and weakens the protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the Economic Espionage Act, which suggests that strict measures are necessary to protect trade secrets. The modification, by not guaranteeing the same level of care, potentially fails to meet this standard.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information to the extent required by law, provided that, subject to and without any waiver of the privileges and immunities of UNHCR , the Recipient will give UNHCR sufficient prior notice of a request for the di sclosure of the Confidential Information in order to allow UNHCR to have a reasonable opportunity to take protective measures or such other action as may be appropriate before any such disclosure is made.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information to the extent they believe it is required by law, and will try to provide UNHCR with some advance notification if practical, before disclosing, allowing UNHCR a potential opportunity to consider possible actions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "required by law" to "they believe it is required by law" introduces subjective interpretation. Removing "sufficient prior notice" and replacing it with "try to provide...some advance notification if practical" weakens the notification obligation. Replacing "a reasonable opportunity to take protective measures or such other action as may be appropriate" with "a potential opportunity to consider possible actions" further diminishes the recipient's responsibility to enable UNHCR's protective actions.
perturbation.law_explanation: This weakens the Recipient's obligation to provide adequate notification to UNHCR, which may hinder UNHCR's ability to respond to subpoenas or court orders effectively. Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45 allows parties to object to subpoenas and seek protective orders. The ambiguous language makes it harder for UNHCR to enforce its right to sufficient notice and thus properly respond to a subpoena.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that undermine UNHCR's ability to enforce its rights, potentially hindering its response to subpoenas or court orders. The original contract language ensured 'sufficient prior notice' and 'a reasonable opportunity to take protective measures'. The change weakens these obligations by stating 'they believe it is required by law' which introduces subjective interpretation. It also replaces 'sufficient prior notice' with 'try to provide...some advance notification if practical,' diminishing the recipient's duty. The phrase 'a potential opportunity to consider possible actions' further reduces the recipient's responsibility to facilitate UNHCR's protective actions. While the scraped snippets discuss the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure generally and subpoena rules (Rule 45), the core contradiction lies in the weakening of the notification obligation, making it difficult for UNHCR to prepare and respond effectively, as highlighted in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees to indem nify UNHCR in respect of any expenses, losses, damages, costs, claims or liability UNHCR may suffer or incur as a result of an act or omission by the Recipient or its 4 employees, consultants and agents in connection with the Confidential Information and the Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will consider indemnifying UNHCR for certain expenses, losses, damages, costs, claims or liability UNHCR purportedly incurs as a potential result of a direct and intentional act or omission by the Recipient or its employees, consultants, and agents directly related to the Confidential Information and the Recipient’s explicitly stated obligations under this Agreement, subject to further negotiation.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "agrees to indemnify" to "will consider indemnifying" weakens the obligation. Adding "purportedly incurs" questions the validity of UNHCR's claims. Inserting "a direct and intentional act" raises the bar for indemnification. Adding "explicitly stated" limits the scope of obligations. The phrase "subject to further negotiation" introduces additional uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification violates the duty of good faith and fair dealing implied in contracts. By making indemnification conditional and subject to further negotiation, the Recipient undermines the reasonable expectations of UNHCR that it will be protected from losses resulting from the Recipient's actions or omissions related to the Confidential Information. The 'consider' clause and 'subject to negotiation' aspects creates ambiguity and undermines the contract's implied duty of good faith.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'agrees to indemnify' to 'will consider indemnifying' introduces significant ambiguity and weakens the original obligation. The phrase 'subject to further negotiation' further undermines the certainty of indemnification. While the provided scraped snippets do not directly address indemnification, the Law Explanation argues that this modification violates the duty of good faith and fair dealing implied in contracts. This weakening of the indemnification obligation, making it conditional and subject to further negotiation, creates a legal ambiguity about the recipient's responsibility, which undermines the implicit expectation of UNHCR to be protected from losses. The 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition states that a legal contradiction happens when an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce, thus resulting in non-compliance with regulatory requirements and leaving legal obligations open to dispute.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall not advertise or otherwise make public the fact that it has a confidential relationship with UNHCR, nor shall the Recipient, in any manner whatsoever use the name, emblem, o r official seal of the United Nations or UNHCR, or any abbreviation of the name of the United Nations or UNHCR in connection with its business or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will generally refrain from actively advertising or publicly disclosing the existence of a confidential relationship with UNHCR without express written consent, and will attempt to avoid using the official name, emblem, or seal of the UN or UNHCR, or common abbreviations thereof, in direct connection with explicit business promotions.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall not" with "will generally refrain" weakens the prohibition. Adding "actively advertising or publicly disclosing…without express written consent" implies that other forms of disclosure might be permissible. Inserting "attempt to avoid" makes using the name/emblem/seal discretionary. The addition of "direct connection with explicit business promotions" narrows the scope of the restriction, making it ambiguous what constitutes a prohibited use.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change could potentially weaken the protection of UNHCR's or the UN's trademarks under the Lanham Act. While the Lanham Act focuses on preventing consumer confusion and unfair competition, the ambiguous language in the modified clause makes it harder to prove trademark infringement or dilution. The clause, as modified, opens the door for arguments that the Recipient's use of the UN/UNHCR name or emblem was not explicitly connected to business promotions or was not likely to cause confusion, weakening the protection afforded by the Lanham Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. The United States, all agencies and instrume
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contract's prohibition against using UNHCR's name or emblem, creating a potential conflict with trademark law. The original text's 'shall not' is replaced with more permissive language like 'will generally refrain' and 'attempt to avoid,' which introduces ambiguity. This ambiguity weakens the ability of UNHCR to prevent unauthorized use of its trademarks. Scraped Law Snippet 1 from 15 U.S. Code § 1114(1)(a) prohibits the use of a 'reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion.' By making the prohibition less strict, the modified contract creates an ambiguity that could allow the Recipient to use UNHCR's trademarks in a way that is 'likely to cause confusion' without explicitly violating the contract. The added qualifiers like 'actively advertising,' 'without express written consent,' and 'direct connection with explicit business promotions' further narrow the scope of the restriction. This makes it more difficult for UNHCR to prove trademark infringement, as the Recipient could argue that their use of the trademark does not fall within these explicitly prohibited categories.
